steps.title: ACUTE KIDNEY INJURY (AKI)
steps.text: Which of the following clinical features in a child should prompt immediate referral to secondary care for possible acute kidney injury?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the presence of any of these signs—swelling, high BP, abnormal urine findings, or reduced urine output (oedema and hypertension, hematuria, proteinuria, or oliguria/anuria) —suggests potential AKI. Early referral is essential to prevent complications.

textChoices.text: Mild abdominal pain and low-grade fever

steps.title: Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
steps.text: Which of the following clinical features in a child should raise suspicion of chronic kidney disease and prompt referral to secondary care?
steps.rationale: Features like chronic anaemia, polyuria, and poor growth (failure to thrive) are red flags for chronic kidney disease (CKD). Early recognition and referral from primary level are crucial to prevent complications.

textChoices.text: Acute onset of itchy rash without systemic signs
Chronic anaemia, polyuria, and failure to thrive
Isolated abdominal colic with normal growth

steps.title: Nephrotic Syndrome
steps.text: A 6-year-old presents with facial puffiness and reduced urine output. Urinalysis shows proteinuria. Which of the following findings most clearly indicates the need for referral to secondary care?
steps.rationale: Nephrotic-range proteinuria (≥3+ on dipstick) is a hallmark of nephrotic syndrome and warrants referral to secondary care for confirmation and treatment. Lesser degrees of proteinuria may be transient or physiologic and don’t necessarily require urgent referral.

textChoices.text: Trace proteinuria with normal blood pressure and no edema
2+ proteinuria with mild lower limb swelling after exercise
≥3+ proteinuria on dipstick, with or without edema
1+ proteinuria with history of recent fever and upper respiratory infection

steps.title: GLOMERULONEPHRITIS / NEPHRITIC SYNDROME
steps.text: A 9-year-old presents with facial puffiness, elevated blood pressure, and decreased urine output. You suspect nephritic syndrome. What is the most appropriate initial step at the primary level?
steps.rationale: In a child with suspected nephritic syndrome and fluid overload, administer frusemide (1–2 mg/kg) stat while beginning ABCDE stabilization.

textChoices.text: Start corticosteroids and observe for 48 hours
Administer frusemide and initiate ABCDE management

steps.title: GLOMERULONEPHRITIS / NEPHRITIC SYNDROME
steps.text: Which of the following symptoms should prompt referral to secondary care in a child with suspected glomerulonephritis?
steps.rationale: The triad of edema, hypertension, and hematuria is characteristic of nephritic syndrome, requiring urgent referral to secondary care.

steps.title: HAEMOLYTIC URAEMIC SYNDROME (HUS)
steps.text: What is the most appropriate action at the primary care level for a child suspected to have Haemolytic Uraemic Syndrome (HUS)?
steps.rationale: At the primary care level, the priority in suspected HUS is to stabilize the child using ABCDE and refer promptly to a higher level of care. Other interventions, including dialysis or disease-specific therapy, are not appropriate at this stage.

textChoices.text: Stabilize the patient using the ABCDE approach and refer

steps.title: HYPERNATRAEMIA
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial fluid for intravenous maintenance in a child with suspected hypernatraemia at primary level?
steps.rationale: Ringer’s lactate or 0.9% normal saline is recommended as initial maintenance fluid before referral. Hypotonic or hypertonic fluids are contraindicated at this stage.

steps.title: HYPERNATRAEMIA
steps.text: A child presents with vomiting, diarrhoea, and signs of severe dehydration. What is the most appropriate action at primary level?
steps.rationale: Severe dehydration and vomiting require IV fluid initiation (Ringer’s lactate or 0.9% saline) and urgent referral to a higher-level facility.

textChoices.text: Start Plan A oral rehydration therapy
Insert IV line and refer after initiating maintenance fluid
Give home fluids and reassess in 24 hours

steps.title: HYPERNATRAEMIA
steps.text: When should a child with suspected hypernatraemia be referred from primary care?
steps.rationale: Referral should be based on clinical signs and history, even without confirmed lab sodium levels, especially if signs suggest hypernatraemia or dehydration.

textChoices.text: If signs of dehydration with history of high fluid losses are present
If serum sodium is confirmed above 145 mmol/L

steps.title: HYPONATREMIA
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in a child with suspected hyponatraemia at primary level?
steps.rationale: Assessment of hydration and prompt referral are key at the primary level; initiating inappropriate therapy without labs may worsen the condition.

steps.title: HYPONATREMIA
steps.text: A child presents with vomiting, diarrhoea, and cannot tolerate oral fluids. What is the correct action at primary level?
steps.rationale: If oral intake is not tolerated, ORS should be given via NGT using Plan B, and the child should be referred promptly.

textChoices.text: Start IV hypotonic fluids and monitor
Insert a nasogastric tube and administer ORS
Wait for labs before starting fluids

steps.title: HYPONATREMIA
steps.text: Which IV fluid is appropriate to start before referring a child with clinical signs of hyponatraemia?
steps.rationale: Normal saline is the fluid of choice before referral. Hypotonic or dextrose-containing fluids may worsen hyponatraemia.

steps.title: HYPERCALCAEMIA
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate action at the primary care level when a child presents with suspected hypercalcaemia?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, the focus is on identifying signs of severity, treating any emergencies, and referring complicated cases. Advanced diagnostics and treatment are not typically performed at this level.

textChoices.text: Conduct thorough clinical assessment and refer if complicated

steps.title: HYPOCALCAEMIA
steps.text: Which of the following is the most appropriate step at the primary care level when a child presents with signs suggestive of hypocalcaemia (e.g. seizures, muscle twitching)?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, management focuses on stabilizing the child if there are emergency symptoms and promptly referring the case. Definitive investigations and treatment should occur at a higher level of care.

textChoices.text: Start oral calcium supplementation and monitor for 24 hours
Refer the patient after giving high-dose vitamin D
Treat presenting emergency symptoms and refer to higher-level care
Prescribe calcium-rich diet and follow up in a week

steps.title: HYPERTENSION IN CHILDREN
steps.text: What is the correct course of action at the primary care level for a child suspected of having hypertension?
steps.rationale: At the primary level, any suspected hypertension in children requires prompt referral to secondary care for comprehensive evaluation and appropriate management.

textChoices.text: Start the child on antihypertensive medication immediately
Observe the child for a few months before taking action
Monitor the blood pressure weekly in the clinic
Refer all suspected cases of hypertension
